content.heading: Stack vs Heap Memory
content.lead: Before ownership, borrowing, or lifetimes make sense ; you need to understand where values actually live in memory. This is the bedrock. Everything else is built on it.

sections.text: JavaScript Hides This. Rust Doesn't.

In JavaScript, you never think about memory. The engine allocates it, and the garbage collector frees it. In Rust, you are always ; implicitly ; making decisions about where data lives. The compiler enforces those decisions.
There are two memory regions that matter:

rows: Must be known at compile time
Can grow or shrink at runtime
Extremely fast ; just moves a pointer
Automatic ; when scope ends, stack unwinds
Must be freed ; Rust's ownership does this

sections.text: What a String Actually Is
This is the most important memory diagram in Rust. A String is not just text ; it's a three-field struct on the stack that points to actual text data on the heap:

Why does this matter? Because when you move or drop a String, three things happen: the stack struct is cleared, the heap data is freed. This is the whole reason ownership exists ; to track exactly who is responsible for freeing that heap allocation.

sections.head: 🔑 The Core Insight
sections.text: Types that live entirely on the stack (i32, bool, char) can be copied cheaply ; just duplicate a few bytes. Types that own heap data (String, Vec) cannot be copied trivially ; they'd create two pointers to the same heap memory, and when both tried to free it, you'd have a double-free crash. This is exactly why Rust invented ownership and move semantics.
Copy Types vs Move Types ; The Fundamental Split

rows: Copied ; both point to same literal

content.heading: One Owner, Always
content.lead: Ownership is Rust's core innovation. It's a set of three compile-time rules that replace garbage collection entirely ; with zero runtime cost.

sections.text: The Three Ownership Rules

sections.head: THE RULES ; memorise these
sections.text: Rule 1: Every value has exactly one owner at any moment. Rule 2: There can only be one owner at a time. Rule 3: When the owner goes out of scope, the value is dropped (freed).
That's it. The entire borrow checker and lifetime system exists to enforce these three rules. Everything else is a consequence of them.
Scope is the Owner's Lifetime

sections.head: 🔁 JS Translation
sections.text: In JavaScript, the GC watches for when no references exist and then (eventually) frees the memory. In Rust, freedom happens immediately and deterministically at the closing brace. No background process, no unpredictable pauses. This is why Rust is used in systems programming ; you know exactly when memory is freed.
Ownership in vaultlog ; the LogEntry
Look at LogEntry ; every field with type String means the struct owns that heap data:
Drop Order ; Rust is Precise
When a struct is dropped, Rust drops its fields in reverse declaration order. This is deterministic and guaranteed. Your LogEntry frees hash first, then previous_hash, then description, then id.
What "Out of Scope" Actually Means
Scope in Rust is defined by curly braces { }. Every block ; function body, if branch, loop body, match arm ; is a scope. Variables are dropped when their enclosing scope closes:

content.heading: Why Values Disappear
content.lead: Move semantics is where most Rust beginners hit their first wall. The compiler rejects code that looks completely normal. Here's exactly why ; and why it's protecting you.

sections.text: The Problem Move Solves

Imagine two variables pointing to the same heap allocation. When both try to free that memory when they go out of scope ; that's a double-free error. In C, it's undefined behaviour. In JavaScript, the GC tracks references to prevent it. In Rust, the compiler makes it impossible to compile.
Move = Transfer of Ownership
Instead of copying the heap data, Rust moves ownership. The original variable becomes invalid ; the compiler prevents you from using it ever again. This is checked at compile time, not runtime:

left.label: COMPILE ERROR ; use after move

right.label: CORRECT ; use the new owner

sections.text: Move into Functions ; the Most Common Surprise
Passing a value into a function moves ownership into that function. After the call, your variable is gone:
Clone ; Explicit Deep Copy
If you genuinely need two independent copies, you call .clone(). This duplicates the heap data too. It's explicit because it's expensive ; Rust wants you to see every heap allocation:
Copy Types ; the Exception
Simple stack-only types implement the Copy trait and are automatically copied on assignment. No move, no loss of original:

sections.head: 🔁 JS Parallel
sections.text: In JavaScript, objects are always passed by reference ; two variables can point to the same object. When you write const b = a for an object, both a and b point to the same thing. This is convenient but causes subtle bugs. Rust's move semantics make ownership explicit ; there is always exactly one owner, and the compiler tells you when you violate that.

content.heading: & and &mut ; Lending Without Giving
content.lead: Moving ownership every time you pass a value to a function would make code unworkable. Borrowing is how you let functions use a value temporarily without transferring ownership.

sections.text: The & Reference ; Immutable Borrow
Putting & in front of a type creates a reference ; a pointer to the value that doesn't take ownership. The original variable remains the owner:
The &mut Reference ; Mutable Borrow
To modify a value through a reference, use &mut. But there's a strict rule: only one mutable reference at a time, ever:
Why &str and &[T] Are References
A &str is literally a reference to a slice of a String's heap data ; a pointer plus a length. This is why you can take a &str of a String without copying anything:

content.heading: The Three Borrow Laws
content.lead: The borrow checker enforces three rules that make Rust's memory safety possible. Violate any one and your code won't compile. Understand all three and the checker becomes a tool, not a wall.

sections.head: THE THREE BORROW RULES
sections.text: Law 1: You can have any number of immutable (&) references ; OR. Law 2: You can have exactly one mutable (&mut) reference ; BUT. Law 3: You cannot have both at the same time.
Law 1 ; Many Immutable Readers ✓
Law 2 ; One Exclusive Writer ✓
Law 3 ; Can't Mix Them ✗

left.label: COMPILE ERROR ; mixed borrows

right.label: FIXED ; borrows don't overlap

sections.text: Why These Rules Exist ; The Real Reason

These aren't arbitrary restrictions. They are the compile-time equivalent of preventing data races. A data race happens when: two or more threads access the same memory, at least one is writing, and there's no synchronisation.

The borrow rules eliminate data races by making the pattern impossible to compile ; even in single-threaded code. This is also why Rust's concurrency story is so strong: the same rules that prevent bugs in single-threaded code prevent race conditions in multi-threaded code automatically.
The Borrow Checker in vaultlog's verify_chain
The chain verification function is a perfect example of correct borrowing:

content.heading: 'a and Why They Exist
content.lead: Lifetimes are the most intimidating Rust syntax. But they're not doing anything new ; they're just making explicit what the compiler already tracks invisibly. Once you see what problem they solve, the syntax becomes obvious.

sections.text: The Problem: Dangling References
A dangling reference is a pointer to memory that has already been freed. In C, this is a silent crash waiting to happen. Rust's lifetime system makes dangling references a compile error:

left.label: DANGLING REFERENCE ; compile error

right.label: VALID ; reference lives within owner

sections.text: Lifetime Annotations ; Naming How Long

Most of the time, the compiler infers lifetimes automatically (called lifetime elision). You only write explicit lifetime annotations when the compiler needs help connecting multiple references in a function signature.
Lifetime syntax looks like generic parameters but with a tick: 'a. You can use any name ; 'a, 'b, 'entry, 'static.
What 'a Actually Means

A lifetime annotation doesn't change how long something lives. It's a constraint you're expressing to the compiler ; "I promise the output reference is valid for at least as long as the input." If your code violates that promise, it won't compile.
'static ; Lives Forever
There's one special lifetime: 'static. It means the reference is valid for the entire program. String literals are 'static because they're baked into the binary:
Lifetimes in Structs
If a struct holds a reference (not an owned value), it needs a lifetime annotation:

sections.head: ✅ Practical Advice
sections.text: In vaultlog, you almost never write explicit lifetime annotations because your structs own their data (String, not &str). Lifetimes become necessary when you write functions that return references, or when structs hold references. Start by using owned types everywhere, and only reach for lifetime annotations when the compiler tells you to. Then read its error message carefully ; it almost always tells you exactly what annotation to add.

content.heading: Shared Behaviour
content.lead: A trait defines a set of methods that a type must implement. It's Rust's equivalent of a TypeScript interface ; but more powerful, because they can have default method implementations and they power the entire generics system.

sections.text: Traits as Contracts
When you write #[derive(Serialize, Debug, Clone)] on LogEntry, you're telling Rust: "auto-implement these three traits for this struct." Each trait is a contract.
Defining Your Own Trait
The Derive Traits ; What They Actually Do

rows: Converts struct to JSON/bytes via serde
Writing entries to the NDJSON file + hashing
Creates struct from JSON/bytes via serde
Reading entries back from the file
When you need an independent copy of an entry

sections.text: Display ; the fmt::Display Trait
The Display trait controls how your type prints with {} in format strings. You implement it manually when you want a human-readable output:

content.heading: <T> and Trait Bounds
content.lead: Generics let you write code that works for multiple types without duplicating it. Trait bounds let you say "this generic T must be able to do at least this." Together, they make Rust's zero-cost abstractions possible.

sections.text: The Problem Generics Solve
Without generics, you'd need a separate function for every type:

left.label: WITHOUT GENERICS ; duplication

right.label: WITH GENERICS ; one function

sections.text: Trait Bounds ; Constraining T
A generic T with no bounds knows nothing about the type ; you can't call any methods on it. Trait bounds tell the compiler what T can do:
Generics in vaultlog ; Vec<LogEntry>
Vec<T> is the most common generic you'll use. It's a growable heap-allocated array that works for any type:
Box<dyn Error> ; Dynamic Dispatch
You've seen this in return types. It's a specific use of generics worth understanding:

sections.head: 🔁 JS Translation
sections.text: Generics in Rust are like TypeScript generics (function first<T>(arr: T[]): T) ; same concept, just stricter. The key difference: Rust generics with trait bounds are monomorphised at compile time ; the compiler generates a separate version of the function for each concrete type it's called with. No runtime overhead, unlike TypeScript generics which are erased.

content.heading: All Five Concepts in One File
content.lead: Every concept from this guide appears together in the vaultlog source. Here's the full annotated storage module ; read it top to bottom and you'll see all five systems interacting in real code.

sections.text: storage.rs ; Fully Annotated
The Ownership Flow of a Full Add Command

items: let description = String::from("Purchased 123 Main St") → description created, owns heap allocation
let entry = LogEntry { description, .. } → description MOVES into LogEntry; LogEntry now owns it
let hash = compute_hash(&entry) → entry is BORROWED; compute_hash reads it, returns a new String
entry.hash = hash → hash (new String) MOVES into the entry struct field
append_entry(&entry) → entry BORROWED; storage serialises it, writes to file
} ← closing brace of add_entry() → entry DROPS; all its String fields freed. One allocation, one free.

sections.text: The Five Concepts ; One Reference Table

rows: You know where data lives and how it's freed
Exactly one owner; freed exactly once on drop
LogEntry owning its String fields; entries returned from functions
description moving into LogEntry; entries moving into Vec
&entry in compute_hash and append_entry; &[LogEntry] in verify_chain
Serialize, Deserialize, Debug, Clone on LogEntry
Vec<LogEntry>, Result<T, E>, Option<T>, filter_map, collect

sections.head: ✅ What to Do Next
sections.text: Return to this guide whenever the compiler rejects your code. Ask: Is this an ownership error? A borrow error? A lifetime error? The compiler error message will contain the answer and often the fix. Over time, you'll stop needing this reference ; these rules become instinctive, and you'll start writing code that the compiler accepts on the first try.
